title: India-Pakistan Indus Canal Water Dispute
description: Following partition, India cut off water flowing to Pakistani canals from the Upper Bari Doab Canal system on 1 April 1948, affecting millions of acres of farmland. The crisis lasted weeks and was resolved by an emergency Inter-Dominion Agreement in May 1948.
source: Gulhati, N.D. (1973). Indus Waters Treaty: An Exercise in International Mediation. Allied Publishers; Wikipedia, Indus Waters Treaty

title: Egypt Protests Ethiopian Nile Projects
description: Egypt formally objects to any upstream water works in Ethiopia that could affect Nile flows, invoking the 1929 Anglo-Egyptian Nile Waters Agreement and demanding recognition of Egypt's historical water rights.
source: Origins, Ohio State University (2013). Who Owns the Nile? https://origins.osu.edu/article/who-owns-nile-egypt-sudan-and-ethiopia-s-history-changing-dam

title: Johnston Plan Negotiations for Jordan River
description: US envoy Eric Johnston conducts multi-year negotiations between Israel and Arab states to allocate Jordan River waters. Despite technical agreement on water quotas, the plan was rejected by the Arab League in 1955 due to political concerns over recognizing Israel.
source: Gleick, P. (1994). Water, War, and Peace in the Middle East. Environment 36(3); KJIS Jordan River Water Dispute study

title: Mekong Committee Established
description: Cambodia, Laos, Thailand, and South Vietnam establish the Committee for Coordination of Investigations of the Lower Mekong Basin (Mekong Committee) under UN auspices, the first institutional framework for Mekong cooperation.
source: Mekong River Commission (1995). Agreement on the Cooperation for the Sustainable Development of the Mekong River Basin. https://www.mrcmekong.org/agreement/

title: Egypt-Sudan Nile Waters Agreement
description: Egypt and Sudan sign a comprehensive water-sharing agreement allocating 55.5 BCM annually to Egypt and 18.5 BCM to Sudan, enabling construction of the Aswan High Dam. Upstream states were excluded from negotiations.
source: Ahram Online (2020). Egypt-Ethiopia Nile water dispute: A timeline. https://english.ahram.org.eg/News/369666.aspx

title: Indus Waters Treaty Signed
description: India and Pakistan, with World Bank mediation, sign the Indus Waters Treaty in Karachi. The treaty allocates the eastern rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to India and the western rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) to Pakistan, resolving a decade of post-partition disputes.
source: World Bank (2018). Fact Sheet: The Indus Waters Treaty 1960. https://www.worldbank.org/en/region/sar/brief/fact-sheet-the-indus-waters-treaty-1960-and-the-world-bank

title: Ethiopia Contests 1929 Nile Agreement Validity
description: Ethiopia officially challenges the validity of the 1929 Anglo-Egyptian Nile Waters Agreement, arguing it was signed without Ethiopian consent and should not bind Ethiopia's use of the Blue Nile and Lake Tana tributaries.
source: Origins, Ohio State University (2013). Who Owns the Nile? https://origins.osu.edu/article/who-owns-nile-egypt-sudan-and-ethiopia-s-history-changing-dam

title: Columbia River Treaty Signed
description: The United States and Canada sign the Columbia River Treaty, providing for construction of three dams in British Columbia for flood control and hydropower. The US secures flood control and pays Canada nearly $65 million; Canada receives half the downstream power benefits.
source: Wikipedia, Columbia River Treaty; High Country News (2024). Renegotiating the Columbia River Treaty. https://www.hcn.org/issues/51-12/reckoning-with-history-renegotiating-the-columbia-river-treaty-six-decades-later/

title: Aswan High Dam Construction Begins with Soviet Aid
description: Egypt begins construction of the Aswan High Dam with Soviet financing after the West withdrew funding. Ethiopia and upstream states protest that they were excluded from the 1959 water allocation framework underpinning the project.
source: Ahram Online (2020). Egypt-Ethiopia Nile water dispute: A timeline. https://english.ahram.org.eg/News/369666.aspx

title: Niger Basin Act of Niamey
description: Nine Niger Basin states sign the Act of Niamey, committing to equitable navigation and economic cooperation over the Niger River, laying the groundwork for the later Niger River Commission and Niger Basin Authority.
countries: Benin, Burkina Faso, Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, Guinea, Mali, Niger, Nigeria
source: FAO FAOLEX. Agreement Concerning the River Niger Commission, 1964. https://www.fao.org/4/w7414b/w7414b06.htm

title: Israel-Syria Jordan River Water War Begins
description: Syria begins diverting the Banias and Hasbani tributaries to prevent water reaching Israel's National Water Carrier, triggering Israeli tank and artillery attacks on Syrian diversion works. Fighting continues until 1967 and is linked to the outbreak of the Six-Day War.
source: Wikipedia, War over Water (Jordan River). https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/War_over_Water_(Jordan_River)

title: River Niger Commission Founded
description: Ten riparian states establish the River Niger Commission at Niamey to promote joint studies and coordinate development of Niger Basin resources, entering into force in 1966.
countries: Benin, Burkina Faso, Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, Guinea, Mali, Niger, Nigeria, Chad
source: IAEA. Niger Basin Authority: Legal and Institutional Frameworks. https://www.iaea.org/sites/default/files/18/10/niger-basin-authority-050514.pdf

title: Six-Day War: Water as a Casus Belli
description: Israel's pre-emptive strike opening the Six-Day War is partly motivated by Arab diversion of Jordan River headwaters. Israeli aircraft destroy Syrian diversion works on the Banias in the weeks before the war. Control of water resources is later secured by Israel's occupation of the Golan Heights and West Bank.
source: Wikipedia, War over Water (Jordan River). https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/War_over_Water_(Jordan_River); Seo, E. KJIS Jordan River Water Dispute study

title: Aswan High Dam Completed
description: Egypt completes the Aswan High Dam, creating Lake Nasser (the world's largest man-made lake). Sudan agrees to resettle 100,000 Nubian residents displaced by the reservoir, and Egypt compensates Sudan for flooded territory.
source: Ahram Online (2020). Egypt-Ethiopia Nile water dispute: A timeline. https://english.ahram.org.eg/News/369666.aspx

title: OMVS Senegal River Basin Organization Founded
description: Senegal, Mali, and Mauritania establish the Organisation pour la mise en valeur du fleuve Sénégal (OMVS), an innovative joint authority that treats the river as shared property, enabling development of Manantali and Diama dams.
source: Wikipedia, OMVS. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Organisation_pour_la_mise_en_valeur_du_fleuve_S%C3%A9n%C3%A9gal

title: Helmand River Treaty: Afghanistan-Iran
description: Afghanistan and Iran sign the Helmand River Water Treaty, allocating Iran 22 cubic meters per second (about 820 MCM/yr) from the Helmand. The treaty is never fully ratified or implemented, causing persistent disputes.
source: Wikipedia, Afghanistan-Iran water dispute. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Afghanistan%E2%80%93Iran_water_dispute

title: Turkey-Syria Euphrates Crisis
description: Turkey and Syria simultaneously begin filling the Keban and Taqba dams during a drought year, dramatically reducing Euphrates flows to Iraq. Iraq deploys troops to the Syrian border and demands Saudi mediation, bringing the two countries close to armed conflict.
source: Climate-Diplomacy (2022). Turkey, Syria and Iraq: Conflict over the Euphrates-Tigris. https://climate-diplomacy.org/case-studies/turkey-syria-and-iraq-conflict-over-euphrates-tigris

title: India Opens Farakka Barrage on Ganges
description: India commissions the Farakka Barrage on the Ganges just upstream of the Bangladesh border, diverting flows to flush Kolkata port. Bangladesh protests that dry-season flows plummet, devastating agriculture and the Sundarbans ecosystem downstream.
source: IWAP (2019). A critical review of the Ganges Water Sharing arrangement. https://iwaponline.com/wp/article/21/2/259/66094/

title: Rhine Salt Pollution Treaty
description: Five Rhine riparian states (Germany, France, Netherlands, Luxembourg, Switzerland) sign the Convention on the Protection of the Rhine against Chloride Pollution, requiring France to reduce salt discharges from Alsace potash mines into the Rhine.
source: Sciencedirect. International co-operation on Rhine water quality 1945-2008. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1474706508001241

title: Egypt's Sadat Threatens War Over Nile
description: Egyptian President Anwar Sadat publicly states that water is the only cause for which Egypt would go to war again, signaling Cairo's existential view of Nile water security amid upstream African development plans.
source: Wikipedia, Water conflict. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_conflict

title: Niger Basin Authority Created
description: Ten Niger riparian states transform the River Niger Commission into the Niger Basin Authority (NBA) at Faranah, Guinea, with a broader mandate covering energy, water, agriculture, transport, and industry coordination.
countries: Benin, Burkina Faso, Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, Guinea, Mali, Niger, Nigeria, Chad
source: IAEA. Niger Basin Authority: Legal and Institutional Frameworks. https://www.iaea.org/sites/default/files/18/10/niger-basin-authority-050514.pdf

title: Iraq Drains Mesopotamian Marshes as Weapon
description: Saddam Hussein begins draining the Mesopotamian Marshes of the Tigris-Euphrates delta, using water as a weapon to punish and displace Marsh Arab (Madan) communities suspected of supporting Iranian forces during the Iran-Iraq War. An estimated 500,000 people are eventually displaced.
source: Wikipedia, Water conflict. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_conflict

title: Turkey-Syria Euphrates Water-PKK Agreement
description: Turkey and Syria broker a political agreement linking water policy to security: Turkey commits to releasing at least 500 m³/s of Euphrates water to Syria, while Syria agrees to stop supporting PKK Kurdish militants operating from Syrian territory.
source: Climate-Diplomacy (2022). Turkey, Syria and Iraq: Conflict over the Euphrates-Tigris. https://climate-diplomacy.org/case-studies/turkey-syria-and-iraq-conflict-over-euphrates-tigris

title: India-Bangladesh Ganges Dispute Worsens
description: Bangladesh formally accuses India of unilateral diversion of Ganges water at the Farakka Barrage, causing desertification of the northwest and saline intrusion in the Sundarbans delta. Bilateral talks repeatedly fail.
source: Wikipedia, Ganges water dispute. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ganges_water_dispute

title: Turkey Cuts Euphrates Flow During Gulf War
description: Turkey cuts off Euphrates River flow for a month to fill Atatürk Dam reservoir, coinciding with Iraq's invasion of Kuwait. Iraq and Syria experience severely reduced water availability during a period of heightened conflict.
source: Climate-Diplomacy (2022). Turkey, Syria and Iraq: Conflict over the Euphrates-Tigris. https://climate-diplomacy.org/case-studies/turkey-syria-and-iraq-conflict-over-euphrates-tigris; Gulf News (2021)

title: Aral Sea Interstate Commission for Water Coordination
description: Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, and Uzbekistan establish the Interstate Commission for Water Coordination of Central Asia (ICWC) to jointly manage Amu Darya and Syr Darya resources, responding to the Aral Sea crisis.
source: Wikipedia, Aral Sea. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aral_Sea

title: Aral Sea Central Asia Budget Pledge
description: Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, Turkmenistan, Tajikistan, and Kyrgyzstan sign an agreement pledging 1% of their national budgets to fund Aral Sea restoration efforts, an unprecedented regional environmental commitment.
source: Wikipedia, Aral Sea. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aral_Sea

title: Israel-Jordan Peace Treaty Water Provisions
description: The Wadi Araba Peace Treaty between Israel and Jordan includes water-sharing provisions allocating Jordan 50 MCM/yr from the Sea of Galilee and assigning Jordan 75% of the Yarmouk River waters, representing the first formal water-sharing agreement between the two states.
source: Stimson Center (2023). Water: A Matter of Cooperation or Conflict Among Jordan, Israel, and Palestine. https://www.stimson.org/2023/water-a-matter-of-cooperation-or-conflict-among-jordan-israel-and-palestine/

title: Mekong River Commission Agreement
description: Cambodia, Laos, Thailand, and Vietnam sign the Agreement on Cooperation for the Sustainable Development of the Mekong River Basin in Chiang Rai, establishing the Mekong River Commission with principles of reasonable and equitable use of waters.
source: MRC (1995). Agreement on Cooperation for Sustainable Development of Mekong River Basin. https://www.mrcmekong.org/agreement/

title: China Begins Manwan Dam on Mekong (Lancang)
description: China completes the first dam (Manwan) in its planned cascade of eight dams on the Upper Mekong (Lancang) in Yunnan Province, alarming downstream states Cambodia, Laos, Thailand, and Vietnam about reduced flows and ecosystem impacts. China proceeds without consulting downstream neighbors.
source: Yale Global (2009). Dams in China Turn the Mekong Into a River of Discord. https://archive-yaleglobal.yale.edu/content/dams-china-turn-mekong-river-discord

title: India-Bangladesh Ganges Water Sharing Treaty
description: Indian PM H.D. Deve Gowda and Bangladeshi PM Sheikh Hasina sign a 30-year Ganges Water Sharing Treaty in New Delhi, the first comprehensive agreement establishing a formula to share dry-season flows at Farakka and recognizing Bangladesh's downstream rights.
source: Wikipedia, Ganges water dispute. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ganges_water_dispute

title: Iraq Calls for Tigris-Euphrates Sharing Agreement
description: Iraq reiterates its call to Syria and Turkey for an 'equitable system' for sharing Tigris and Euphrates waters within one year, amid continued tensions over Turkey's GAP project that has reduced downstream flows by up to 50%.
source: University of Colorado Law Review. Water Conflict and the Tigris-Euphrates Water Basin. https://scholar.law.colorado.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1662&context=celj

title: Danube River Protection Convention Enters Force
description: The Convention on Cooperation for the Protection and Sustainable Use of the Danube River enters into force, establishing the International Commission for the Protection of the Danube River (ICPDR) involving 14 states and the EU.
countries: Germany, Austria, Czech Republic, Slovakia, Hungary, Slovenia, Croatia, Bosnia, Serbia, Romania, Bulgaria, Moldova, Ukraine
source: ICPDR (2018). The flow of Danube cooperation. https://www.icpdr.org/publications/flow-danube-cooperation-history-shared-responsibility

title: Nile Basin Initiative Established
description: Nine of ten Nile riparian states establish the Nile Basin Initiative (NBI) with World Bank backing, creating the first basin-wide institutional framework to promote cooperative development and management of Nile water resources.
countries: Ethiopia, Uganda, Kenya, Tanzania, Rwanda, Burundi, DRC, Sudan, Egypt
source: Nile Basin Initiative. Cooperative Framework Agreement. https://nilebasin.org/about-us/cooperative-framework-agreement

title: Cochabamba Water War, Bolivia
description: Violent protests erupt in Cochabamba, Bolivia, after the privatization of the city's water system leads to price spikes. At least 9 people are killed and hundreds injured. The government reverses privatization and renationalizes the water supply.
source: ReliefWeb (2017). Editor's Pick: 10 Violent Water Conflicts. https://reliefweb.int/report/world/editor-s-pick-10-violent-water-conflicts

title: India's Baglihar Dam Dispute with Pakistan
description: Pakistan formally objects to India's construction of the Baglihar Dam on the Chenab River (a western river allocated to Pakistan under the IWT), claiming it violates treaty specifications regarding design and storage capacity, initiating Indus Waters Commission proceedings.
source: World Bank (2018). Fact Sheet: The Indus Waters Treaty 1960. https://www.worldbank.org/en/region/sar/brief/fact-sheet-the-indus-waters-treaty-1960-and-the-world-bank

title: Iraq War: Water Infrastructure Destruction
description: During the US-led invasion of Iraq, water infrastructure including pumping stations and treatment plants is damaged or destroyed, leaving millions in Baghdad and southern Iraq without safe drinking water. The UN warns of a humanitarian crisis.
source: Pacific Institute, Water Conflict Chronology 2003 entries. https://pacinst.org/water-conflict-chronology/

title: Zambezi Watercourse Commission Agreement (ZAMCOM)
description: Eight Zambezi riparian states (Angola, Botswana, Malawi, Mozambique, Namibia, Tanzania, Zambia, Zimbabwe) sign the ZAMCOM Agreement establishing the Zambezi Watercourse Commission for integrated management of the basin.
countries: Angola, Botswana, Malawi, Mozambique, Namibia, Tanzania, Zambia, Zimbabwe
source: UNECE, ZAMCOM Agreement and Institution. https://unece.org/sites/default/files/2022-12/Presentation%2010_ZAMCOM_National_Workshop_Presentation_Final.pdf

title: Kazakhstan Kok-Aral Dam Saves North Aral Sea
description: Kazakhstan, with World Bank financing, completes the Kok-Aral dike and dam separating the North and South Aral Sea, successfully raising water levels in the northern section. Fish populations begin recovering after three decades of collapse.
source: NASA Science. World of Change: Shrinking Aral Sea. https://science.nasa.gov/earth/earth-observatory/world-of-change/aral-sea/; NPR Aral Sea interactive. https://apps.npr.org/aral-sea-shrinking-map/

title: Euphrates-Tigris Initiative for Cooperation (ETIC)
description: Turkey, Syria, and Iraq establish the Euphrates-Tigris Initiative for Cooperation (ETIC) as a Track II platform for scientists and water managers to promote cross-border water dialogue, reflecting a limited rapprochement after decades of tension.
source: Climate-Diplomacy (2022). Turkey, Syria and Iraq: Conflict over the Euphrates-Tigris. https://climate-diplomacy.org/case-studies/turkey-syria-and-iraq-conflict-over-euphrates-tigris

title: World Bank Neutral Expert Rules on Baglihar Dam
description: World Bank-appointed Neutral Expert Professor Raymond Lafitte rules that India's Baglihar Dam largely conforms to the Indus Waters Treaty but orders minor design modifications to pondage capacity, a partial win for Pakistan.
source: World Bank (2018). Fact Sheet: The Indus Waters Treaty 1960. https://www.worldbank.org/en/region/sar/brief/fact-sheet-the-indus-waters-treaty-1960-and-the-world-bank

title: Nile CFA Negotiations Stall
description: Negotiations on the Nile Basin Cooperative Framework Agreement stall at the ministerial level over Egypt and Sudan's insistence on maintaining their historical water security rights (Article 14b), versus upstream states' demands for equitable utilization.
countries: Egypt, Sudan, Ethiopia, Uganda, Kenya, Tanzania, Rwanda, Burundi, DRC
source: ReliefWeb (2017). Editor's Pick: 10 Violent Water Conflicts. https://reliefweb.int/report/world/editor-s-pick-10-violent-water-conflicts

title: Niger Basin Water Charter Adopted
description: Heads of State of the Niger Basin Authority adopt the Niger Basin Water Charter at a summit in Niamey, promoting equitable and sustainable water resources management and entering into force in 2010.
countries: Benin, Burkina Faso, Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, Guinea, Mali, Niger, Nigeria, Chad
source: IAEA. Niger Basin Authority: Legal and Institutional Frameworks. https://www.iaea.org/sites/default/files/18/10/niger-basin-authority-050514.pdf

title: Turkey-Iraq and Turkey-Syria Water MoUs
description: Turkey signs Memoranda of Understanding on water management with both Iraq and Syria, and jointly agrees with Syria to build a dam on the shared Orontes River in Hatay province, marking a diplomatic thaw in the Tigris-Euphrates basin.
source: Climate-Diplomacy (2022). Turkey, Syria and Iraq: Conflict over the Euphrates-Tigris. https://climate-diplomacy.org/case-studies/turkey-syria-and-iraq-conflict-over-euphrates-tigris

title: Mekong Low Water Levels Crisis
description: Water levels in the lower Mekong River fall to critically low levels during the dry season, and downstream states Cambodia, Vietnam, and Laos blame Chinese upstream dams for disrupting natural flows and affecting fisheries and agriculture.
source: Yale Global (2009). Dams in China Turn the Mekong Into a River of Discord. https://archive-yaleglobal.yale.edu/content/dams-china-turn-mekong-river-discord

title: Nile Cooperative Framework Agreement Opened for Signature
description: Six upstream Nile states (Ethiopia, Rwanda, Tanzania, Uganda, Kenya, Burundi) sign the Cooperative Framework Agreement in Entebbe, establishing equitable utilization principles for the Nile. Egypt and Sudan refuse to sign, rejecting any revision of their historical water allocations.
countries: Ethiopia, Uganda, Kenya, Tanzania, Rwanda, Burundi, Egypt, Sudan
source: Nile Basin Initiative. Cooperative Framework Agreement. https://nilebasin.org/about-us/cooperative-framework-agreement

title: Ethiopia Announces Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam
description: Ethiopia announces construction of the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam (GERD) on the Blue Nile in Benishangul-Gumuz region without prior consultation with Egypt or Sudan. Egypt and Sudan protest that the dam threatens their Nile water security.
source: GIGA Hamburg. The Political Deadlock on the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam. https://www.giga-hamburg.de/en/publications/giga-focus/the-political-deadlock-on-the-grand-ethiopian-renaissance-dam

title: Xayaburi Dam Controversy, Laos
description: Laos begins construction of the Xayaburi Dam on the Mekong mainstream despite objections from Vietnam and Cambodia that environmental impact studies are inadequate. Thailand agrees to buy 95% of generated power. The episode highlights weaknesses in MRC's enforcement capacity.
source: Climate-Diplomacy. Dam projects and disputes in the Mekong River Basin. https://climate-diplomacy.org/case-studies/dam-projects-and-disputes-mekong-river-basin

title: Ethiopia Ratifies Nile CFA
description: Ethiopia becomes the first country to ratify the Nile Basin Cooperative Framework Agreement, deepening the split between upstream and downstream states over Nile governance.
source: Nile Basin Initiative. Cooperative Framework Agreement. https://nilebasin.org/about-us/cooperative-framework-agreement

title: Ukraine Blocks North Crimean Canal
description: Following Russia's annexation of Crimea, Ukraine blocks the North Crimean Canal, which had supplied 85% of Crimea's fresh water from the Dnipro River. The blockade causes severe water shortages for Crimea's 2 million residents.
source: Wikipedia, Water conflict. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_conflict

title: Egypt-Ethiopia-Sudan Declaration of Principles
description: Egypt, Sudan, and Ethiopia sign the Declaration of Principles on the GERD in Khartoum, establishing priority for downstream electricity purchase, a damage mitigation framework, and drought/low-flow procedures. However, the agreement lacks binding filling and operation rules.
source: Arab Center Washington DC (2020). Water Conflict Between Egypt and Ethiopia. https://arabcenterdc.org/resource/water-conflict-between-egypt-and-ethiopia-a-defining-moment-for-both-countries/

title: Pakistan Requests Arbitration on Kishanganga and Ratle Dams
description: Pakistan asks the World Bank to constitute an ad hoc Court of Arbitration to adjudicate disputes over India's Kishanganga and Ratle hydropower projects on the western Jhelum and Chenab rivers, alleging treaty violations. India seeks a Neutral Expert instead.
source: World Bank (2018). Fact Sheet: The Indus Waters Treaty 1960. https://www.worldbank.org/en/region/sar/brief/fact-sheet-the-indus-waters-treaty-1960-and-the-world-bank

title: Mekong Reaches Lowest Level in 100 Years
description: Water levels in the Mekong fall to their lowest point in more than a century even during the monsoon season. Vietnam, Cambodia, and Thailand accuse China's cascade of 11 dams of disrupting seasonal flooding cycles critical to agriculture and fisheries.
source: Wikipedia, Water conflict. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_conflict

title: Cauvery Water Dispute: India Inter-State Violence
description: Violent protests and riots erupt in Karnataka and Tamil Nadu following the Supreme Court of India's order to increase Cauvery River water releases to Tamil Nadu. Shops are burned, curfews imposed, and over 100 arrests made across both states.
source: Pacific Institute, Water Conflict Chronology. https://pacinst.org/water-conflict-chronology/; ReliefWeb (2017). Editor's Pick: 10 Violent Water Conflicts

title: India Threatens to Block Indus Tributaries After Pulwama Attack
description: Following the Pulwama terrorist attack, Indian Prime Minister Modi threatens to divert water flowing from Indian-controlled rivers to Pakistan under the Indus Waters Treaty. Pakistan's foreign minister warns this would be considered an act of war.
source: Wikipedia, Indus Waters Treaty. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indus_Waters_Treaty

title: Ethiopia Second Nile CFA Ratification Fails
description: Efforts to bring Egypt and Sudan into the Nile Cooperative Framework Agreement collapse as GERD negotiations deadlock. The AU facilitates talks but the parties remain far apart on filling speed and drought protocols.
source: GIGA Hamburg. The Political Deadlock on the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam. https://www.giga-hamburg.de/en/publications/giga-focus/the-political-deadlock-on-the-grand-ethiopian-renaissance-dam

title: Egypt Brings GERD Dispute to UN Security Council
description: Egypt submits a formal letter to the UN Security Council protesting Ethiopia's plans to begin first filling of GERD without agreement. Ethiopia responds that it has no legal obligation to seek Egypt's approval, calling Egypt's position a 'colonial mindset.'
source: Ahram Online (2020). Egypt-Ethiopia Nile water dispute: A timeline. https://english.ahram.org.eg/News/369666.aspx

title: Ethiopia Begins First GERD Filling
description: Ethiopia begins the first filling of the GERD reservoir in July 2020 despite no agreement with Egypt or Sudan on operating rules. Egyptian officials call it an existential threat and Ethiopian PM Abiy Ahmed says millions could be mobilized if war is necessary.
source: Sciencedirect (2024). Impacts of the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam on the Nile. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0022169424003469

title: Mexico-US Colorado/Rio Grande Water Debt Tensions
description: Mexico fails to meet its deadline to deliver approximately 300 million cubic meters of water to the US under the 1944 treaty on the Rio Grande. Farmers in Chihuahua revolt against the government's attempts to comply, seizing control of a dam. US farmers in Texas face water shortages.
source: ArcGIS Blog (2020). Water Conflicts in International Rivers. https://www.esri.com/arcgis-blog/products/arcgis-living-atlas/water/water-conflicts-in-international-rivers

title: Kyrgyzstan-Tajikistan Isfara River Armed Clash
description: A three-day armed border conflict erupts on April 28 between Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan over a disputed water reservoir and pumping station on the Isfara River in Batken province. At least 55 people are killed, hundreds injured, and thousands evacuated before a ceasefire. The deadliest border incident since Central Asian independence.
source: Reuters (2021). Kyrgyz and Tajik security forces clash at border in water dispute. https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/kyrgyz-tajik-security-forces-clash-border-water-dispute-2021-04-29/; Wikipedia, 2021 Kyrgyzstan-Tajikistan clashes

title: Ethiopia Second GERD Filling Proceeds Without Agreement
description: Ethiopia proceeds with the second filling of GERD (July 2021) despite failing to reach agreement with Egypt and Sudan at the AU-mediated talks. Egypt again brings the matter to the UN Security Council; the UNSC urges parties to resume AU talks.
source: GIGA Hamburg. The Political Deadlock on the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam. https://www.giga-hamburg.de/en/publications/giga-focus/the-political-deadlock-on-the-grand-ethiopian-renaissance-dam

title: Afghanistan Kamal Khan Dam Opens on Helmand
description: Afghanistan completes and opens the Kamal Khan Dam on the Helmand River, giving Afghanistan full control of Helmand flows for the first time. Iran protests the dam threatens its water rights under the 1973 treaty; Taliban authorities deny a major impact.
source: Cscr.pk. Assessing the Helmand River Dispute. https://cscr.pk/explore/themes/politics-governance/assessing-the-helmand-river-dispute/

title: Kyrgyzstan-Tajikistan Large-Scale Clashes
description: A more deadly round of Kyrgyzstan-Tajikistan border conflict erupts in September 2022, leaving over 100 people dead and more than 100,000 Kyrgyz civilians displaced. Water access and irrigation canal disputes again feature as underlying triggers.
source: CACI Analyst (2025). Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan Resolve Final Border Dispute. https://www.cacianalyst.org/publications/analytical-articles/item/13868-kyrgyzstan-and-tajikistan-resolve-final-border-dispute-a-historic-but-fragile-peace.html

title: Afghanistan-Iran Helmand Tensions Intensify
description: Iran-Afghanistan Helmand River tensions escalate after Taliban takeover. Iran complains it received far less water than the 820 MCM/yr agreed in the 1973 treaty; Iran's President Raisi publicly warns Afghanistan to comply with treaty obligations.
source: Water-Peace-Security (2022). Emerging dynamics for conflict and cooperation between Iran and the Taliban over the Helmand River. https://waterpeacesecurity.org/info/blog-09-09-2022

title: Ethiopia Third GERD Filling
description: Ethiopia completes the third phase of GERD reservoir filling in August 2022, storing an additional large volume. The Nile flow downstream at Sudan's Roseires and Egypt's Aswan shows no significant change in this phase, but tensions remain over long-term drought scenarios.
source: Sciencedirect (2024). Impacts of the GERD on the Nile. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0022169424003469

title: India-Pakistan PCA Rules on Indus Waters Treaty Jurisdiction
description: The Permanent Court of Arbitration rules unanimously that it has jurisdiction to hear Pakistan's case on India's Kishanganga and Ratle dams, and that India cannot unilaterally suspend proceedings. India rejects the ruling as 'illegal and void.'
source: Lowy Institute (2026). The Indus Treaty verdict: When water outlasts war. https://www.lowyinstitute.org/the-interpreter/indus-treaty-verdict-when-water-outlasts-war

title: Israel Attacks Gaza Water Infrastructure 250+ Times
description: During the 2023 Gaza conflict, Israeli forces attack Palestinian water sources and infrastructure in the West Bank and Gaza over 250 times in five years according to data compiled by the Pacific Institute. The attacks leave Gaza's water system near total collapse by late 2023.
source: Pacific Institute Water Conflict Chronology Fact Sheet (2024). https://pacinst.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/Water-Conflict-Chronology_Fact-Sheet.pdf

title: Iran-Afghanistan Helmand Deadly Clashes
description: Tensions over Helmand River water rights between Iran and the Taliban escalate into armed incidents along the border in May 2023, resulting in multiple deaths and injuries on both sides.
source: Pacific Institute Water Conflict Chronology Fact Sheet (2024). https://pacinst.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/Water-Conflict-Chronology_Fact-Sheet.pdf

title: India Nagarjunasagar Dam Police Clash
description: Hundreds of police from Andhra Pradesh clash with Telangana state police guarding the Nagarjunasagar Dam on the Krishna River in a long-standing interstate water dispute over reservoir management and irrigation releases.
source: Pacific Institute Water Conflict Chronology Fact Sheet (2024). https://pacinst.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/Water-Conflict-Chronology_Fact-Sheet.pdf

title: Kyrgyzstan-Tajikistan Final Border Treaty
description: Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan officially resolve all outstanding border and water-sharing disputes with a landmark treaty signed in Bishkek, demarcating the full 972 km shared border including the previously disputed Vorukh exclave and Isfara River water distribution arrangements.
source: CACI Analyst (2025). Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan Resolve Final Border Dispute. https://www.cacianalyst.org/publications/analytical-articles/item/13868-kyrgyzstan-and-tajikistan-resolve-final-border-dispute-a-historic-but-fragile-peace.html

title: GERD Inaugurated, Egypt Protests at UN
description: Ethiopia officially inaugurates the GERD on September 9, 2024. Egypt formally registers its objection at the UN Security Council on the same day, calling the dam a unilateral action threatening Egyptian water security and sovereignty.
source: African Futures ISS (2025). Water wars: has GERD reset Africa's hydropolitics? https://futures.issafrica.org/blog/2025/Water-wars-has-GERD-reset-Africas-hydropolitics

title: India Suspends Indus Waters Treaty After Pahalgam Attack
description: India suspends the Indus Waters Treaty in 2025 following the Pahalgam terror attack attributed to Pakistan-linked groups. Pakistan's FM Bilawal Bhutto warns of war over water access; the PCA reaffirms the treaty remains in force and its jurisdiction stands.
source: Lowy Institute (2026). The Indus Treaty verdict: When water outlasts war. https://www.lowyinstitute.org/the-interpreter/indus-treaty-verdict-when-water-outlasts-war

title: US-Canada Columbia River Treaty Renegotiation Stalls
description: Decade-long renegotiations of the Columbia River Treaty collapse as Trump's trade war with Canada injects geopolitical tension. Trump references the treaty as leverage for broader trade negotiations, threatening flood-control cooperation in the Pacific Northwest.
source: New York Times (2025). How the Columbia River Treaty Got Tangled in Trump's Feud With Canada. https://www.nytimes.com/2025/05/13/technology/trump-canada-columbia-river-treaty.html

title: Kyrgyzstan-Tajikistan-Uzbekistan Summit on Water
description: Presidents of Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, and Uzbekistan meet in Khujand and jointly declare all outstanding territorial and water-access disputes resolved, launching a new era of Central Asian water cooperation.
source: CACI Analyst (2025). Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan Resolve Final Border Dispute. https://www.cacianalyst.org/publications/analytical-articles/item/13868-kyrgyzstan-and-tajikistan-resolve-final-border-dispute-a-historic-but-fragile-peace.html

title: Colorado River Compact Signed
description: Representatives of seven US Colorado River basin states (Arizona, California, Colorado, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah, Wyoming) sign the Colorado River Compact in Santa Fe, dividing 15 million acre-feet of annual river flow equally between the Upper and Lower Basins, with Mexico excluded.
source: Audubon Society (2024). The Colorado River Compact at 100. https://www.audubon.org/news/the-colorado-river-compact-100; US Bureau of Reclamation. Colorado River Compact 1922. https://www.usbr.gov/lc/region/g1000/pdfiles/crcompct.pdf

title: US-Mexico Treaty on Rio Grande and Colorado River
description: The United States and Mexico sign a landmark treaty governing the Rio Grande, Colorado, and Tijuana Rivers, allocating 1.5 million acre-feet of Colorado River water annually to Mexico and establishing water exchange obligations on the Rio Grande.
source: Wikipedia, Colorado River Compact. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Colorado_River_Compact; ArcGIS Blog (2020). Water Conflicts in International Rivers.

title: Israel National Water Carrier Operational
description: Israel's National Water Carrier begins pumping water from the Sea of Galilee (Lake Kinneret) southward, diverting approximately 400 MCM/year. Arab states view this as water theft and accelerate plans to divert Jordan headwaters, triggering the 1964-67 Water War.
source: Wikipedia, War over Water (Jordan River). https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/War_over_Water_(Jordan_River)

title: Rhine Chemical Pollution Crisis
description: A series of industrial chemical spills from Swiss and German factories kill millions of fish along the Rhine, prompting widespread public outrage. The disaster catalyzes the creation of the International Commission for the Protection of the Rhine (ICPR) in 1950 to take on stronger enforcement powers.
source: Sciencedirect. International co-operation on Rhine water quality 1945-2008. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1474706508001241

title: Sandoz Chemical Spill Poisons Rhine
description: A warehouse fire at Sandoz chemical plant in Basel, Switzerland, flushes 30 tonnes of pesticides into the Rhine, killing nearly all aquatic life for 400 km downstream into Germany and the Netherlands. The disaster leads to the 1994 Rhine Action Programme and dramatically accelerates transboundary water governance.
source: Sciencedirect. International co-operation on Rhine water quality 1945-2008. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1474706508001241

title: Rhine Action Programme Achieves Salmon Return
description: The Rhine Action Programme (RAP), launched by riparian states in 1987 following the Sandoz disaster, achieves its landmark goal of restoring salmon to the Rhine after decades of absence. The programme is considered one of the most successful international river restoration initiatives.
source: Sciencedirect. International co-operation on Rhine water quality 1945-2008. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1474706508001241

title: Soviet Union Aral Sea Diversion Decision
description: The Soviet government under Khrushchev implements large-scale irrigation diversions from the Amu Darya and Syr Darya rivers to support cotton and other agriculture across Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, and Turkmenistan, deliberately sacrificing the Aral Sea to achieve agricultural targets.
countries: Soviet Union (Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, Turkmenistan, Tajikistan, Kyrgyzstan)
source: Columbia University. Introduction - The Aral Sea Crisis. https://www.columbia.edu/~tmt2120/introduction.htm; NASA Science (2026). World of Change: Shrinking Aral Sea.

title: Aral Sea Splits into North and South
description: By 1989-1990, the Aral Sea's water level drops so severely from Soviet irrigation diversions that it splits into the North Aral Sea (Kazakhstan) and South Aral Sea (Uzbekistan). The fishing industry that once supported 40,000 people collapses entirely.
source: NPR Aral Sea interactive. https://apps.npr.org/aral-sea-shrinking-map/; Wikipedia, Aral Sea. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aral_Sea

title: India-Bangladesh Joint Rivers Commission
description: Indian PM Indira Gandhi and Bangladeshi leader Sheikh Mujibur Rahman sign the Indo-Bangladesh Treaty of Friendship, establishing a Joint Rivers Commission (JRC) to cooperate on water sharing, flood control, irrigation, and cyclone management.
source: Wikipedia, Ganges water dispute. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ganges_water_dispute

title: India-Bangladesh Farakka Barrage Declaration
description: India and Bangladesh issue a joint declaration to resolve the water-sharing dispute over the Farakka Barrage before its commencement of operations. A 1975 interim agreement follows, allowing India to operate feeder canals for short test periods.
source: Wikipedia, Ganges water dispute. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ganges_water_dispute

title: Danube Cyanide Spill from Romania Gold Mine
description: A containment dam at the Aurul gold mine in Baia Mare, Romania, ruptures releasing 100,000 cubic metres of cyanide-contaminated water into the Somes River and ultimately the Danube, killing aquatic life across Hungary, Yugoslavia, and downstream into the Black Sea.
source: ICPDR (2018). The flow of Danube cooperation. https://www.icpdr.org/publications/flow-danube-cooperation-history-shared-responsibility

title: Mauritania-Senegal Border Water Conflict
description: Ethnic clashes between Mauritanians and Senegalese erupt along the Senegal River border, triggered by disputes over pastoralist access to agricultural land and river water. Hundreds are killed and over 250,000 people displaced in both directions across the river.
source: Pacific Institute, Water Conflict Chronology. https://pacinst.org/water-conflict-chronology/

title: ZACPLAN Zambezi River System Action Plan
description: Eight southern African states initiate the ZACPLAN process under UNEP to manage Zambezi River resources collectively, representing the first multilateral framework for the basin and laying groundwork for the eventual ZAMCOM agreement.
countries: Angola, Botswana, Malawi, Mozambique, Namibia, Tanzania, Zambia, Zimbabwe
source: Wikipedia, Zambezi River System Action Plan. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zambezi_River_System_Action_Plan

title: Anglo-Egyptian Nile Waters Agreement
description: Britain, representing Uganda, Kenya, Tanganyika, and Sudan, signs an agreement with Egypt granting Egypt the right to veto upstream Nile projects and allocating 55.5 BCM annually to Egypt and 18.5 BCM to Sudan. Upstream African states are not consulted.
source: Ahram Online (2020). Egypt-Ethiopia Nile water dispute: A timeline. https://english.ahram.org.eg/News/369666.aspx

title: Israel Declares Independence: Arab States Threaten Jordan Water
description: Following Israeli independence, Arab states threaten to restrict Jordan River flows as a means of economic pressure. The Johnston Plan negotiations begin in the early 1950s in an attempt to reach a technical water-sharing agreement.
source: Gleick, P. (1994). Water, War, and Peace in the Middle East. Environment 36(3)

title: Israel Captures West Bank Water Resources
description: Following Israel's capture of the West Bank in the Six-Day War, Israel issues Military Order 158 prohibiting Palestinians from building any new water infrastructure without army permits, which are effectively unobtainable. Israel gains control of Mountain Aquifer and Jordan River access.
source: Stimson Center (2023). Water: A Matter of Cooperation or Conflict Among Jordan, Israel, and Palestine. https://www.stimson.org/2023/water-a-matter-of-cooperation-or-conflict-among-jordan-israel-and-palestine/

title: Oslo II Accords West Bank Water Provisions
description: The Oslo II Interim Agreement between Israel and the PLO includes detailed provisions on Palestinian water rights in the West Bank, granting Palestine an additional 28.6 MCM/year and establishing a Joint Water Committee, but maintaining Israeli predominance over water allocation.
source: Stimson Center (2023). Water: A Matter of Cooperation or Conflict Among Jordan, Israel, and Palestine. https://www.stimson.org/2023/water-a-matter-of-cooperation-or-conflict-among-jordan-israel-and-palestine/

title: Lauca River Diversion: Chile-Bolivia Ongoing Dispute
description: Bolivia persistently protests Chile's diversion of Lauca River waters (begun in 1962) for irrigation in Chile's Atacama region. Bolivia breaks off diplomatic relations with Chile partly over water and territory, a dispute that persists into the 21st century.
source: Pacific Institute, Water Conflict Chronology. https://pacinst.org/water-conflict-chronology/

title: Lake Chad Basin Commission Established
description: Cameroon, Chad, Niger, and Nigeria establish the Lake Chad Basin Commission (LCBC) in Fort-Lamy to coordinate development and management of Lake Chad and its basin, one of Africa's earliest multilateral water bodies.
source: FAO. International River Basin Treaty Compendium; Nile Basin experience comparison

title: Lake Chad Near Collapse Triggers Regional Crisis
description: Lake Chad has shrunk by 90% since the 1960s due to irrigation extraction, drought, and climate change. Water conflicts proliferate among fishing communities of Nigeria, Niger, Chad, and Cameroon. The LCBC launches an emergency restoration project with World Bank and African Development Bank support.
source: Pacific Institute, Water Conflict Chronology. https://pacinst.org/water-conflict-chronology/

title: Gulf War: Iraqi Forces Discharge Oil into Gulf Waterways
description: Iraqi forces deliberately release millions of barrels of crude oil into the Persian Gulf and set fire to Kuwaiti oil wells, polluting critical water supplies and desalination plants used by Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, and Gulf states.
source: Pacific Institute, Water Conflict Chronology. https://pacinst.org/water-conflict-chronology/

title: Lesotho Highlands Water Project: South Africa-Lesotho
description: The Lesotho Highlands Water Project (LHWP) delivers first significant water transfers to water-scarce South Africa's Gauteng province from Lesotho highlands via transboundary tunnels, in exchange for royalties paid to Lesotho. A model example of water trade.
source: African Futures ISS (2025). Water wars: has GERD reset Africa's hydropolitics? https://futures.issafrica.org/blog/2025/Water-wars-has-GERD-reset-Africas-hydropolitics

title: India Builds Farakka Barrage Permanently Operational
description: India makes the Farakka Barrage permanently operational to maintain navigability of Kolkata port via the Bhagirathi-Hooghly channel, permanently diverting significant dry-season Ganges flows away from Bangladesh. Bangladesh protests this as an act of economic hostility.
source: IWAP (2019). A critical review of the Ganges Water Sharing arrangement. https://iwaponline.com/wp/article/21/2/259/66094/

title: India Suspends Permanent Indus Commission Talks
description: After the Uri terrorist attack, India suspends the biannual Permanent Indus Commission meetings with Pakistan, halting the formal cooperative mechanism established under the 1960 treaty. Cross-border shooting incidents near Indian rivers escalate tensions.
source: Wikipedia, Indus Waters Treaty. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indus_Waters_Treaty

title: Colorado River Reaches Sea for First Time in Decades
description: The Colorado River has not reached the Sea of Cortez (Gulf of California) for most of the preceding decades due to upstream US and Mexican water extraction. Environmental flows negotiations between the US and Mexico under the 1944 Treaty continue.
source: Audubon Society (2024). The Colorado River Compact at 100. https://www.audubon.org/news/the-colorado-river-compact-100

title: Mekong Committee Suspended During Vietnam War
description: The Mekong Committee suspends operations as the Vietnam War engulfs the basin, with Cambodia under Khmer Rouge control. The committee survives in reduced form, demonstrating that even armed conflict cannot entirely halt transboundary river cooperation.
source: Wikipedia, Water conflict. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_conflict; MRC Agreement background

title: Bangladesh Liberation War: India Diverts Brahmaputra Flows
description: During the Bangladesh liberation war, water infrastructure along the Brahmaputra and Ganges becomes a strategic concern as millions of refugees flee to India. Post-war agreements on joint river management become central to India-Bangladesh relations.
source: ETH Zurich. Water Issues between Nepal, India and Bangladesh. https://www.files.ethz.ch/isn/118874/SR95.pdf

title: Guinea Joins OMVS Senegal River Basin Authority
description: Guinea joins the Organisation pour la mise en valeur du fleuve Sénégal (OMVS) as its fourth member state, completing the basin's institutional framework by including the river's main headwaters state and enhancing cooperative management.
source: Wikipedia, OMVS. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Organisation_pour_la_mise_en_valeur_du_fleuve_S%C3%A9n%C3%A9gal

title: Aswan High Dam Sediment Deprivation Dispute
description: Egypt faces growing scientific concern over Nile delta erosion and loss of agricultural fertility as the Aswan High Dam traps sediment that historically enriched downstream farmland. Egypt disputes Ethiopian upstream development partly on grounds it would further reduce available nutrients.
source: Origins, Ohio State University (2013). Who Owns the Nile? https://origins.osu.edu/article/who-owns-nile-egypt-sudan-and-ethiopia-s-history-changing-dam

title: Burundi Signs Nile CFA
description: Burundi signs the Nile Basin Cooperative Framework Agreement, bringing the total of signatories to six upstream states. The CFA remains contentious as Egypt and Sudan continue to withhold agreement.
countries: Burundi, Ethiopia, Uganda, Kenya, Tanzania, Rwanda
source: Nile Basin Initiative. Cooperative Framework Agreement. https://nilebasin.org/about-us/cooperative-framework-agreement

title: Taliban Takeover Threatens Helmand Treaty Compliance
description: Following the Taliban's seizure of power in Afghanistan (August 2021), Iran faces uncertainty about Helmand River treaty compliance. The Taliban publicly commits to honoring the 1973 Helmand treaty but lacks technical capacity to manage water infrastructure.
source: Water-Peace-Security (2022). Emerging dynamics for conflict and cooperation between Iran and the Taliban over the Helmand River. https://waterpeacesecurity.org/info/blog-09-09-2022

title: China Opens Mekong Water Releases During Drought
description: In response to downstream protests and diplomatic pressure, China agrees in 2020 to share real-time data on upstream Mekong dam operations and releases water from its Yunnan reservoirs to alleviate drought conditions in Lower Mekong riparian states.
countries: China, Cambodia, Laos, Thailand, Vietnam, Myanmar
source: Wikipedia, Water conflict. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_conflict

title: Uzbekistan 2024 Water Emergency Regime
description: Uzbekistan President Mirziyoyev declares an 'emergency water conservation regime' and launches a national plan including farmer education, innovation programs, and financial incentives for water-saving technologies, responding to deepening Aral Sea crisis and climate stress.
source: NPR Aral Sea interactive (2024). https://apps.npr.org/aral-sea-shrinking-map/

title: Rhine Convention for Protection Enters Force
description: The Convention for the Protection of the Rhine (Bern Convention) is upgraded and the 1994 Rhine Action Programme agreed, building on the 1986 Sandoz disaster response to achieve river ecosystem restoration targets.
source: Sciencedirect. International co-operation on Rhine water quality 1945-2008. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1474706508001241

title: India-Nepal Mahakali Treaty Disputes
description: India and Nepal dispute the implementation of the 1996 Mahakali Treaty provisions on the Sarada Dam and Pancheshwar Project on the Mahakali River. Nepal's parliament accuses India of not fulfilling treaty obligations on water sharing and infrastructure funding.
source: ETH Zurich. Water Issues between Nepal, India and Bangladesh. https://www.files.ethz.ch/isn/118874/SR95.pdf

title: China Lancang-Mekong Cooperation Mechanism Proposed
description: China proposes a 'Mekong River community of common destiny' in April 2015, aimed at institutionalizing Chinese leadership in Mekong governance alongside the MRC. Critics see it as China seeking to undermine the existing MRC framework.
countries: China, Cambodia, Laos, Thailand, Vietnam, Myanmar
source: Climate-Diplomacy. Dam projects and disputes in the Mekong River Basin. https://climate-diplomacy.org/case-studies/dam-projects-and-disputes-mekong-river-basin

title: Sudan Splits from Egypt on GERD Negotiations
description: Sudan distances itself from Egypt's position on the GERD, refusing to co-sign an Arab League resolution supporting Egypt and Sudan's historical Nile water rights. Sudan signals openness to the dam for hydropower benefits, fracturing the Egypt-Sudan bloc.
source: Ahram Online (2020). Egypt-Ethiopia Nile water dispute: A timeline. https://english.ahram.org.eg/News/369666.aspx

title: Uganda Ratifies Nile CFA
description: Uganda becomes the fourth country to ratify the Nile Cooperative Framework Agreement in August 2019, marking continued consolidation of upstream riparian states under the new legal framework that challenges Egypt and Sudan's colonial-era allocations.
source: Nile Basin Initiative. Cooperative Framework Agreement. https://nilebasin.org/about-us/cooperative-framework-agreement

title: Israel-Palestine West Bank Water Access Denied
description: Reports document ongoing Israeli restriction of Palestinian access to West Bank water, with Palestinians consuming only 73 litres/day (below WHO minimum of 100) compared to 240 l/day for Israeli settlers. Permit denials for Palestinian water infrastructure remain at near-100% rate.
source: Stimson Center (2023). Water: A Matter of Cooperation or Conflict Among Jordan, Israel, and Palestine. https://www.stimson.org/2023/water-a-matter-of-cooperation-or-conflict-among-jordan-israel-and-palestine/

title: South Sudan Accedes to Nile CFA
description: South Sudan accedes (ratifies) to the Nile Basin Cooperative Framework Agreement in 2024, expanding the upstream bloc and further marginalizing Egypt's and Sudan's historical veto position over Nile development.
countries: South Sudan, Ethiopia, Uganda, Kenya, Tanzania, Rwanda, Burundi
source: Nile Basin Initiative. Cooperative Framework Agreement. https://nilebasin.org/about-us/cooperative-framework-agreement

title: Sudan Nile White Nile Water Dispute
description: Sudan and Egypt fail to reach a joint position on the Nile Cooperative Framework Agreement after Egypt freezes NBI membership in protest of the CFA signings. Sudan withholds its official support for Egypt's Arab League resolution, marking the first significant split in the Egypt-Sudan axis.
source: Ahram Online (2020). Egypt-Ethiopia Nile water dispute: A timeline. https://english.ahram.org.eg/News/369666.aspx

title: Inter-American Water Rights: US-Mexico Colorado River Salinity
description: Decades of US irrigation drainage return highly saline water to the Colorado River, devastating Mexican farms in the Mexicali Valley. The US and Mexico negotiate Minute 242 to the 1944 Treaty, committing the US to deliver Colorado River water at no more than 115 ppm salinity above Mexican levels.
source: Pacific Institute, Water Conflict Chronology. https://pacinst.org/water-conflict-chronology/; US Bureau of Reclamation, Colorado River resources

title: Mosul and Haditha Dams Built on Tigris-Euphrates
description: Iraq completes the Mosul Dam on the Tigris (1984) and Haditha Dam on the Euphrates (1985-1987) for hydropower and water storage. Turkey and Syria claim the dams were built without adequate notification under international water law.
source: Wikipedia, Water conflict. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_conflict

title: GERD Geological Survey Begins
description: Ethiopia's government surveys the Benishangul-Gumuz region east of the Sudan border as a potential site for a major dam on the Blue Nile. The survey is conducted without notification to Egypt or Sudan, presaging the 2011 dam announcement.
source: Ahram Online (2020). Egypt-Ethiopia Nile water dispute: A timeline. https://english.ahram.org.eg/News/369666.aspx

title: Lancang-Mekong Dam Cascade Alarms Downstream
description: China completes the fourth dam in its Lancang cascade (Dachaoshan). The UN Environment Programme and Asian Institute of Technology warn that China's plan for eight dams on the Mekong poses 'a considerable threat' to downstream ecosystems and fisheries supporting 60+ million people.
countries: China, Vietnam, Cambodia, Laos, Thailand, Myanmar
source: Yale Global (2009). Dams in China Turn the Mekong Into a River of Discord. https://archive-yaleglobal.yale.edu/content/dams-china-turn-mekong-river-discord

title: Nile CFA Enters into Force with 5 Ratifications
description: The Nile Basin Cooperative Framework Agreement formally enters into force after receiving its sixth ratification (South Sudan), creating a new legally binding institutional framework for the Nile while Egypt and Sudan remain outside.
countries: Ethiopia, Uganda, Tanzania, Rwanda, Burundi, South Sudan
source: Nile Basin Initiative. Cooperative Framework Agreement. https://nilebasin.org/about-us/cooperative-framework-agreement

title: Uzbekistan Expands Irrigated Land Amid Aral Crisis
description: Despite the Aral Sea crisis, Uzbekistan continues to direct 90% of its water to agriculture and has expanded irrigated land by 43% since 1991, with 70% devoted to cotton. This threatens the tenuous recovery of the North Aral and creates tensions with Kazakhstan over Syr Darya flows.
source: NPR Aral Sea interactive (2024). https://apps.npr.org/aral-sea-shrinking-map/
